beats.narration: Cracks, pores, undercut — every interruption in a material's normal structure is a discontinuity. But is it a defect? Not yet.

payload.title: FLAW ≠ DEFECT
payload.sub: The word changes with the contract, not the metal.

beats.narration: Here's the ladder. A discontinuity becomes a defect only when it's evaluated against the acceptance criteria and found rejectable.

payload.heading: When does it become a defect?

beats.narration: Picture the same undercut on two different jobs. One code accepts it; a stricter code rejects it. Same metal — different verdicts.
That's why your job is to find the indication, describe it accurately, and hand a clean record to whoever evaluates it.
So keep the rule front and center. The metal gives you a discontinuity. Only the criteria can make it a defect.

payload.sub: The metal gives you a discontinuity — only the criteria can make it a defect.
